title: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 — Overview
text: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 (BNS) replaced the Indian Penal Code 1860 with effect from 1 July 2024. It represents a comprehensive overhaul of Indian criminal law. Key changes: (1) New offences added — organised crime (Section 111), terrorism (Section 113), and petty organised crime (Section 112); (2) Community service introduced as a punishment for minor offences; (3) Murder (Section 101 BNS, was Section 302 IPC) — death or imprisonment for life; (4) Culpable homicide not amounting to murder (Section 105 BNS) — graduated punishment; (5) Rape (Section 63 BNS, was Section 375 IPC) — expanded definition of consent; (6) Sedition abolished as an offence; (7) New offence of 'acts endangering sovereignty, unity and integrity of India' (Section 152); (8) Stricter provisions against organised crime networks; (9) Gender-neutral provisions expanded. BNS retains the fundamental structure of the IPC but uses Indian terminology — 'nyāya' (justice) replaces 'penal' in the title, reflecting IKS influence.

title: BNS 2023 — Key Sections
text: Key sections of BNS 2023: Section 3 — General explanations and definitions. Section 4 — Punishments: (a) death; (b) imprisonment for life; (c) imprisonment — rigorous or simple; (d) forfeiture of property; (e) fine; (f) community service. Section 8 — Solitary confinement. Section 15 — Act done by person of unsound mind (mens rea defence). Section 18 — Act of a child under 7 years. Section 22 — Mental illness defence. Section 34 — Acts done by several persons in furtherance of common intention. Section 63 — Rape (minimum 10 years, maximum life). Section 64 — Punishment for rape (aggravated cases — death/life). Section 101 — Murder (death or life imprisonment). Section 105 — Culpable homicide not amounting to murder. Section 109 — Abetment of suicide. Section 111 — Organised crime. Section 113 — Terrorist act. Section 115 — Voluntarily causing grievous hurt. Section 131 — Wrongful restraint. Section 303 — Theft. Section 308 — Extortion. Section 318 — Cheating. Section 351 — Criminal intimidation.

title: Indian Penal Code 1860 — Overview
text: The Indian Penal Code 1860 (IPC) was the general penal code of India until replaced by the BNS 2023 on 1 July 2024. Drafted by Lord Macaulay's Law Commission (1837), it was based on English common law principles but adapted for Indian conditions. Structure: (1) Chapter I — Introduction and definitions; (2) Chapter II — General explanations (actus reus, mens rea, consent, necessity); (3) Chapter III — Punishments (Section 53 — five types: death, transportation/life imprisonment, imprisonment, forfeiture, fine); (4) Chapters IV-XXIII — Specific offences. Key IPC principles still relevant to pre-July 2024 cases: Section 84 — unsound mind defence; Section 85 — intoxication; Sections 96-106 — private defence; Sections 107-120 — abetment; Sections 120A-B — criminal conspiracy; Sections 299-304 — homicide; Section 375 (pre-amendment 2013) — rape definition. IPC cases remain relevant precedents even after BNS because (a) pending cases continue under IPC, and (b) BNS substantially retains IPC structures.

title: Constitution of India — Fundamental Rights (Part III)
text: Part III of the Constitution of India guarantees Fundamental Rights against state action. Key Articles: Article 12 — Definition of 'State' (includes government, Parliament, state legislatures, local authorities, and instrumentalities of government). Article 13 — Laws inconsistent with or in derogation of Fundamental Rights are void. Article 14 — Equality before law; equal protection of laws. Article 15 — Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth. Article 16 — Equality of opportunity in public employment. Article 17 — Abolition of untouchability. Article 19 — Six freedoms: (a) speech and expression; (b) assembly; (c) association; (d) movement; (e) residence; (f) occupation. Article 20 — Protection against double jeopardy, self-incrimination, ex post facto laws. Article 21 — Right to life and personal liberty. Article 21A — Right to education (6-14 years). Article 22 — Protection against arbitrary arrest and detention. Articles 23-24 — Right against exploitation. Articles 25-28 — Right to freedom of religion. Articles 29-30 — Cultural and educational rights. Article 32 — Right to constitutional remedies (Dr Ambedkar called it 'heart and soul of Constitution').

title: Article 14 — Equality Before Law
text: Article 14: 'The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.' Two concepts: (1) 'Equality before law' — negative concept from English common law: no special privileges, equal subjection of all persons to the ordinary law. (2) 'Equal protection of laws' — positive concept from the US 14th Amendment: likes must be treated alike; the state may make reasonable classification but not arbitrary discrimination. The 'reasonable classification' test (from State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar, 1952): classification must (a) be founded on an intelligible differentia; (b) have a rational nexus between the differentia and the object of the Act. The doctrine of 'arbitrariness' as a standalone ground of Article 14 violation: E.P. Royappa v. State of Tamil Nadu (1974) — 'equality is antithetic to arbitrariness.' Article 14 strikes at procedure as well as substance. Maneka Gandhi (1978) created the 'golden triangle' of Articles 14, 19, 21 — any law curtailing life and liberty must satisfy all three.

title: Article 21 — Right to Life and Personal Liberty
text: Article 21: 'No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.' Originally interpreted narrowly (A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras, 1950). Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) transformed Article 21 — 'procedure established by law' must be 'just, fair and reasonable', not merely procedurally valid. This introduced substantive due process into Indian law. Judicial expansion of Article 21: Right to livelihood (Olga Tellis v. BMC, 1985); Right to health and medical care (Paschim Banga v. State of West Bengal, 1996); Right to education (Unni Krishnan v. State of AP, 1993 — later operationalised by Article 21A); Right to privacy (Puttaswamy v. Union of India, 2017 — 9-judge bench); Right to die with dignity (Common Cause v. Union of India, 2018 — passive euthanasia); Right to shelter (Shantistar Builders v. Narayan Totame, 1990); Right to a clean environment (Subhash Kumar v. State of Bihar, 1991); Protection from sexual harassment at workplace (Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan, 1997). Article 21 cannot be suspended even during Emergency (44th Amendment — Minerva Mills impact).

title: Articles 32 and 226 — Writ Jurisdiction
text: Article 32 — Right to move Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Five writs: (1) Habeas Corpus — produce the body; challenges illegal detention. (2) Mandamus — command; directs a public authority to perform a public duty. (3) Prohibition — prevents lower court/tribunal from exceeding jurisdiction. (4) Certiorari — quashes decisions made in excess of jurisdiction or with error on face of record. (5) Quo Warranto — challenges a person's right to hold public office. Article 32 is itself a Fundamental Right — cannot be suspended except during Emergency. Article 226 — High Court writ jurisdiction, broader than Article 32 (writs can be issued for 'any other purpose' too, not just Fundamental Rights). Public Interest Litigation (PIL) developed through Article 32 by Justices Bhagwati and Krishna Iyer from 1979 — opened courts to third-party standing. S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (1981) — liberalised standing; any person with 'sufficient interest' can file PIL. PIL used for: prisoner rights (Hussainara Khatoon, 1979), bonded labour (Bandhua Mukti Morcha, 1984), environmental protection (M.C. Mehta cases), corruption (Vineet Narain v. Union of India, 1997).

title: Indian Contract Act 1872 — Key Provisions
text: The Indian Contract Act 1872 governs contracts in India (excluding agricultural contracts). Essential elements of a valid contract (Section 10): (1) Offer and acceptance (Sections 3-9); (2) Lawful consideration (Sections 23-25) — adequate, not past; (3) Capacity of parties (Section 11) — major, sound mind, not disqualified; (4) Free consent (Section 14) — free from coercion, undue influence, fraud, misrepresentation, mistake; (5) Lawful object (Section 23) — not forbidden by law, immoral, or opposed to public policy; (6) Not declared void. Landmark provisions: Section 27 — agreements in restraint of trade void; Section 28 — agreements in restraint of legal proceedings void; Sections 68-72 — quasi-contracts (restitution); Section 73 — damages for breach (Hadley v. Baxendale rule); Section 74 — liquidated damages and penalty. The Specific Relief Act 1963 (amended 2018) governs specific performance. IKS connection: Arthashastra Book III on contracts (rna/ṛṇa — debt contracts) anticipates many Contract Act provisions — particularly on consideration, capacity, and undue influence (Arthashastra III.1-12).

title: Indian Evidence Act 1872 — Key Provisions
citation: Act No. 1 of 1872 (replaced by Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023)
text: The Indian Evidence Act 1872 governed admissibility and proof in court proceedings. Replaced by Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023 (BSA) from 1 July 2024. Key provisions: Section 3 — Definitions: 'fact', 'relevant', 'proved', 'disproved', 'not proved'. Section 5 — Evidence may be given of facts in issue and relevant facts only. Section 17 — Admission defined. Section 24 — Confession caused by inducement, threat or promise inadmissible. Section 25 — Confession to police officer inadmissible. Section 27 — Information given by accused to police admissible if it leads to discovery of fact ('discovery rule'). Section 32 — Dying declaration as exception to hearsay rule. Section 45 — Expert evidence. Section 101 — Burden of proof on party asserting fact. Section 102 — Burden of proof in criminal cases: prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt. Section 113B — Presumption as to dowry death (reverse burden). The BSA 2023 updates electronic evidence provisions and the definition of 'document' to include electronic records, bringing the law current with digital evidence challenges.

title: Code of Criminal Procedure 1973 — Key Provisions
text: The Code of Criminal Procedure 1973 (CrPC) governed criminal procedure. Replaced by Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 (BNSS) from 1 July 2024. Key CrPC provisions: Section 41 — Arrest without warrant (grounds); Section 50 — Grounds of arrest to be communicated (constitutional right); Section 57 — Person arrested not to be detained more than 24 hours; Section 154 — First Information Report (FIR) registration; Section 161 — Examination of witnesses by police; Section 164 — Magistrate's examination of accused (confession must be voluntary); Section 197 — Sanction for prosecution of public servants; Section 313 — Examination of accused; Section 360 — Order to release on probation (Prāyaścitta equivalent); Section 374-401 — Appeals; Section 482 — High Court's inherent powers. BNSS 2023 key changes: mandatory videography of crime scenes; 60/90 day challan filing deadline; audio-visual recording of statements of women victims; zero FIR (can be filed at any police station regardless of jurisdiction).
